text: I’ll inspect the fixture app’s manifests and use the available bomly tooling to get the vulnerability list before changing versions. Then I’ll update only the dependency files needed, run the root test target, and document each package in `FIXES.md`.

The repo is a large Python fixture app under `fixtures/service`, with untracked instruction files already present. I’m narrowing this to dependency manifests and the bomly scan surface now so the remediation list is complete rather than based on manual guesses.

The unrestricted scan matches the runtime scan: 11 vulnerable packages, all Python, and every one has a fixed version. Some fixes are major upgrades, especially Flask/Werkzeug, so I’m checking the existing build flow before editing the pins.

text: Before locking edits, I’m checking that the fixed versions bomly recommends are actually installable in this environment. If a recommended future version is not available from the package index here, I’ll use the highest installable fixed version only if it still closes every reported advisory.

The fixed versions are present in the package index. I’m updating the input manifest first, then I’ll regenerate the compiled lock so transitive dependencies like `mako`, `idna`, and `cryptography` resolve cleanly instead of hand-editing stale pins.
The first patch missed because I matched the wrong package name spelling in one line. I’ve got the exact manifest layout now and am applying the scoped version changes.
The vulnerable direct pins are updated in `requirements.in`. Next I’m compiling `requirements.txt` with pip-tools so the generated file reflects resolver-compatible transitive updates.

The repo’s documented pip-tools version is not compatible with the current pip API in this environment. I’m switching the compile helper venv to a newer pip-tools version; this only affects regeneration, not the app dependency set.

The resolver found compatibility blockers from existing direct dependencies, not vulnerabilities: `Flask-Caching==2.0.2` excludes Flask 3, and `Flask-SQLAlchemy==2.5.1` is too old for the fixed Flask/Werkzeug line. I’m bumping only the direct compatibility pins needed to allow the security fixes to resolve.

tool: /bin/bash -lc "/tmp/bomly-fix-check/bin/pip index versions Flask-Caching | sed -n '1,40p' && /tmp/bomly-fix-check/bin/pip index versions Flask-SQLAlchemy | sed -n '1,60p' && /tmp/bomly-fix-check/bin/pip index versions Flask-Migrate | sed -n '1,40p'"

text: I’m choosing the smallest compatibility step where possible: Flask-SQLAlchemy 3.0.5 keeps SQLAlchemy 1.4 support, while Flask-Caching needs a current 2.x release for Flask 3. This keeps the blast radius smaller than moving the database layer to SQLAlchemy 2 unless the resolver forces it.

The next resolver blocker is `pybluemonday==0.0.14`, which pins `cffi` to the 1.x series while fixed `cryptography` requires 2.x. I’m checking whether `pybluemonday` has a compatible release; if not, that would be a real no-version-fix conflict to call out.

tool: /bin/bash -lc "/tmp/bomly-fix-check/bin/pip index versions pybluemonday | sed -n '1,80p' && /tmp/bomly-fix-check/bin/pip index versions cffi | sed -n '1,50p'"

/bin/bash -lc "/tmp/bomly-fix-check/bin/pip install --dry-run 'cryptography==48.0.1' 'cffi==1.17.1' 2>&1 | sed -n '1,120p'; /tmp/bomly-fix-check/bin/pip install --dry-run 'cryptography==46.0.6' 'cffi==1.17.1' 2>&1 | sed -n '1,120p'; /tmp/bomly-fix-check/bin/pip install --dry-run 'cryptography==47.0.0' 'cffi==1.17.1' 2>&1 | sed -n '1,80p'"

text: `pybluemonday` has no release that permits `cffi` 2.x, so a fixed `cryptography` pin cannot coexist with the existing sanitizer dependency. Since `cryptography` enters only through the optional `PyMySQL[rsa]` extra, I’m removing that extra while still upgrading PyMySQL to the fixed version; that removes the vulnerable transitive package instead of carrying an unresolvable vulnerable pin.

The compile succeeded, but pip-compile reused old transitive pins for `idna` and `mako`. I’m rerunning with explicit upgrade targets for the vulnerable transitive packages so the lock actually reflects the fixes.

text: The `attrs` lock is updated. I’m rerunning the test target; at this point failures should be narrower application compatibility issues rather than dependency import gaps.

Tests are now reaching application code. Flask 3 removed `app.session_cookie_name`; I’m patching the app’s custom session interface to read the configured `SESSION_COOKIE_NAME`, which is the supported Flask 3 path and preserves existing behavior.
I found three references in the custom session implementation. I’m adding a small helper for the cookie name and replacing all three uses, keeping the change localized.
The Flask 3 session-cookie compatibility patch is in place. I’m rerunning the root tests to catch the next issue.

Werkzeug 3 also stopped accepting bytes for cookie values. The signer still returns bytes, so I’m decoding the signed session id before calling `set_cookie`; `unsign` already handles the incoming string path.
I’m keeping the signer helper unchanged because other callers may rely on bytes. The session interface will normalize just the cookie value it passes to Werkzeug.
The cookie value normalization is patched. I’m rerunning tests again; this should move past session setup.
The suite is progressing now instead of failing globally. There are a handful of remaining test failures; I’m waiting for the full failure summary before making the next targeted change.

Remaining failures are all redirect `Location` formatting: Flask/Werkzeug now leave relative redirects as relative, while this app’s tests and previous behavior expect absolute `http://localhost/...` locations. I’m checking the app’s response setup and will restore the previous absolute-location behavior globally.

The app subclasses Flask centrally, so I can restore absolute redirect locations there instead of touching each redirect call. I’m verifying the response class hook exists in the installed Werkzeug/Flask stack before patching.

tool:
/bin/bash -lc "cd fixtures/service && .venv/bin/python - <<'PY'
from flask import Response
print(hasattr(Response, 'autocorrect_location_header'))
print(Response.autocorrect_location_header)
PY"

text: The hook is available and defaults to `False` in the upgraded stack. I’m adding a tiny response subclass with `autocorrect_location_header = True` and wiring it into `CTFdFlask`.
Absolute redirect behavior is restored at the app response-class level. I’m rerunning `make test`; if this passes, I’ll do a final bomly scan and write `FIXES.md`.
Only one failure has appeared so far in the rerun. I’m waiting for the final summary so I can see whether it is still redirect formatting or something else.

The last failure is URL-encoding of the `next` query value. Flask’s current URL building leaves slashes unescaped inside query values here; I’m restoring the old encoded form for login redirects by quoting `request.full_path` at the decorator boundary.

The remaining failing path comes from visibility decorators rather than auth itself. I’m adding a local helper in the decorator modules to build `?next=` redirects with the old percent-encoded query value, then using it for login/register/team redirects that pass `request.full_path`.
The decorator redirects now encode `next` explicitly. I’m patching the same single page-auth redirect in `views.py` for consistency, then I’ll rerun the required tests.
